jokes.punchline: Jerry agrees he's 'a dandy' and 'a real fancy boy' — adopting this humiliating identity to protect Kramer's tenancy.
jokes.explanation: 'He's a real fancy boy' is great period-specific insult humor delivered with deadpan acceptance. The image of Jerry Seinfeld calling himself a 'fancy boy' to a super is a strong comedic visual.

jokes.text: Maybe this isn't my coat. / Yeah, all right, you're not fancy. / No. He's very fancy. 'Want me. Love me. Shower me with kisses.'
jokes.setup: Jerry breaks character, claiming maybe the coat isn't his; Silvio starts to believe him.
jokes.punchline: Kramer jumps back in with 'No. He's very fancy' and reprises the narcissistic celebrity monologue — adding 'shower me with kisses' to the previous version.
jokes.explanation: The callback on 'Want me. Love me.' with the new addition of 'Shower me with kisses' escalates the celebrity narcissism monologue. Kramer forcing Jerry back into the 'fancy boy' role is well-played cringe comedy.

jokes.text: Jerry, where'd you get it? That's his coat. / No, it's not. It's mine. I'm a fancy boy.
jokes.setup: Joe Mayo arrives and recognizes his coat on Jerry.
jokes.punchline: Jerry's desperate 'No, it's not. It's mine. I'm a fancy boy' — committing to the bit even as it collapses around him.
jokes.explanation: Jerry fully committing to 'I'm a fancy boy' as a defense against coat-theft accusations is a great escalating cringe beat. The desperation behind the claim makes it funnier than if it were planned.

jokes.text: If that is not his coat, who's coat is it? / It's Joe Mayo's coat. / Who's Joe Mayo? / It must be the man that's sleeping with your wife.
jokes.setup: The coat ownership dispute is unraveling in front of Silvio.
jokes.punchline: The full chain: the coat belongs to Joe Mayo → Who's Joe Mayo? → the man sleeping with your wife. All the plot threads collide simultaneously to expose everything at the worst possible moment.
jokes.explanation: The episode's structural climax and one of its strongest moments. The rapid-fire question chain — 'Who's Joe Mayo? / It must be the man sleeping with your wife' — is a perfect Seinfeld convergence joke where every subplot collides in the worst way possible. The structure is flawless: Silvio asks who Joe Mayo is, and the answer is simultaneously accurate and catastrophic. Maximum plot efficiency, maximum comic impact. One of the episode's top moments.

jokes.text: Somebody took my European carryall. / Your what? / The black leather thing with a strap. / You mean a purse? / Yes, a purse. I carry a purse.
jokes.setup: In the chaos following the Silvio confrontation, Jerry's European carryall has been stolen.
jokes.punchline: Jerry, after an entire episode of insisting 'It's European,' immediately tells the cop 'Yes, a purse. I carry a purse.' — complete capitulation under pressure.
jokes.explanation: The running gag's perfect resolution. Under crisis conditions, Jerry abandons all pretense: 'Yes, a purse. I carry a purse.' The episode-long denial collapses in one sentence. This is how you resolve a running gag — with complete, unconditional reversal at the worst moment. Among the episode's top three jokes. Highly quotable, structurally impeccable.

jokes.text: So Silvio ambushed Joe Mayo? / Yeah, he's waiting inside his apartment for him with a sock full of pennies. / Should have had a reverse peephole.
jokes.setup: Jerry and Elaine recap what happened to Joe Mayo.
jokes.punchline: 'Should have had a reverse peephole' — the episode's central absurdist invention perfectly deployed as the moral of the story, AND the callback to 'jack me with a sock full of pennies' from the opening of the subplot.
jokes.explanation: The episode's thematic capstone. Two major running elements — the reverse peephole and the 'sock full of pennies' threat — collide in the most satisfying way possible. Kramer's paranoid invention turns out to be completely justified; the sock full of pennies threat was real all along, just aimed at Joe Mayo rather than Kramer. 'Should have had a reverse peephole' works as both comic callback and perfect ironic moral. Among the episode's absolute best moments.

jokes.text: Yeah, I saw Jerry wearing his. He looked like a bit of a dandy.
jokes.setup: Elaine asks why Puddy ditched the fur coat.
jokes.punchline: Puddy took fashion cues from Jerry wearing the fur — the man in the coat as a cautionary spectacle has scared Puddy straight.
jokes.explanation: A satisfying final callback — Puddy's fur is resolved because Jerry's performance as a 'dandy' was convincing enough to make the actual fur-wearer reconsider. The loop is closed: Elaine's coat complaint is resolved by the very act that caused all the chaos. Puddy using 'dandy' picks up the exact word from the confrontation scene.

jokes.text: Check it out. Eight ball. 'You got a question? You ask the 8 Ball.'
jokes.setup: Puddy has replaced the fur coat with a new coat that has an 8-Ball on it.
jokes.punchline: The 8-Ball coat — Puddy's solution to giving up the fur is not to get a normal coat but to get one with a giant Magic 8-Ball on it. His fashion instincts have not improved.
jokes.explanation: A fantastic episode-ending joke. Puddy's solution to the fur coat problem is a coat with an 8-Ball on it — equally attention-grabbing, equally strange, but completely different. The visual gag is strong, and Puddy's deadpan pride in it is perfect character comedy. 'You got a question? You ask the 8 Ball' implies Puddy fully intends to use the coat as a decision-making tool.

jokes.text: You're gonna wear this all the time? / All signs point to yes.
jokes.setup: Elaine is horrified by the 8-Ball coat.
jokes.punchline: 'All signs point to yes' — Puddy answering in 8-Ball language, speaking through the coat he's wearing. A perfect final line.
jokes.explanation: The episode's single best joke and one of the finest final lines in a Seinfeld episode. 'All signs point to yes' achieves perfection: it's an 8-Ball phrase used as a straight answer, it's delivered in Puddy's deadpan, it closes the Puddy/Elaine fashion subplot, it demonstrates that Puddy has fully committed to living through the 8-Ball's wisdom, and it ends the episode on maximum laugh. The economy is perfect (five words), the character integration is perfect (only Puddy), the structure is perfect (setup → punchline with no excess), and it's instantly quotable. This is the anchor for the episode's top score.

tmdb_overview: Elaine gets into a hassle regarding fur; Jerry stops carrying a wallet, while George suffers from his overstuffed one; Kramer keeps Newman from being evicted.
insight: Solid character work sustains a 70-point episode despite thin standout moments.
